subject: Forensic Medicine
question: A 45-year-old male with a history of working in a battery recycling plant presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent tremors, difficulty with coordination, and occasional episodes of slurred speech. He mentions that these symptoms have been gradually worsening over the past year. On examination, you observes involuntary coarse tremor predominantly in the hands, which intensifies when he attempts to perform tasks or extend his arms. Given the patient's occupational history and clinical presentation, you suspect chronic exposure to a heavy metal. Which of the following heavy metal poisonings is most likely associated with the characteristic tremor observed in this patient?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Mercury

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A . Lead poisoning typically presents with gastrointestinal symptoms, anemia, neuropathy, and cognitive dysfunction , but not with the fine tremor’s characteristic of mercury exposure.
  • • Option
  • • A
  • • Lead poisoning
  • • gastrointestinal symptoms, anemia, neuropathy, and cognitive dysfunction
  • • Option C. Arsenic poisoning can cause skin changes, peripheral neuropathy, and gastrointestinal symptoms , but it does not typically present with tremors.
  • • Option
  • • C.
  • • Arsenic poisoning
  • • skin changes, peripheral neuropathy, and gastrointestinal symptoms
  • • Option D. Thallium poisoning is known for causing severe hair loss (alopecia), painful neuropathies, and gastrointestinal symptoms, but not the specific type of tremor associated with mercury poisoning.
  • • Option
  • • D.
  • • Thallium poisoning
  • • causing severe hair loss

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Intention tremors (Danbury tremors/shaking palsy)
  • ➤ Intention tremors (Danbury tremors/shaking palsy)
  • ➤ It occurs first in the hands , then progresses to the lips and tongue , and finally involves the arms and legs . Tremor is moderately coarse and is interspersed by jerky movements. The patient may not display much tremor during an accustomed job, but if he is being observed, he may begin to shake violently. In the advanced stage, the person is unable to dress himself, write legibly or walk properly. They are also called hatter’s shakes or glass blower’s shakes, as they are common in persons working with mercury in glass-blowing and hat industries. The most severe form of tremors is known as concussion mercurilis.
  • ➤ It occurs first in the hands , then progresses to the lips and tongue , and finally involves the arms and legs .
  • ➤ occurs first in the hands
  • ➤ lips and tongue
  • ➤ arms and legs
  • ➤ Tremor is moderately coarse and is interspersed by jerky movements. The patient may not display much tremor during an accustomed job, but if he is being observed, he may begin to shake violently.
  • ➤ In the advanced stage, the person is unable to dress himself, write legibly or walk properly. They are also called hatter’s shakes or glass blower’s shakes, as they are common in persons working with mercury in glass-blowing and hat industries.
  • ➤ The most severe form of tremors is known as concussion mercurilis.

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Opium - Toxic part : Unripe fruit capsule, latex juice.
  • ➤ Opium - Toxic part
  • ➤ Latex is obtained by lacerating (‘scoring’) the immature seed pods; the latex leaks out and dries to a sticky brown residue. This is scraped off the fruit. Seeds are non-poisonous and are called ‘khaskhas’ which constitutes a condiment in cooking. Active Principles - The latex juice of opium has about 25 alkaloids, divided into two groups: Phenanthrene derivatives (main narcotic constituents) and Benzyl-isoquinolone derivatives (no significant CNS effects).

subject: Forensic Medicine
question: A 28-year-old male presents to the emergency department with agitation, mydriasis, and profuse sweating. He reports a history of substance abuse and mentions recent use of a white powdery substance at a party. On examination, his vital signs are elevated, and he is experiencing palpitations. What is the most likely substance responsible for his current clinical presentation?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Cocaine

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A . MDMA ( Ecstasy ): MDMA (Methylene-dioxy-methamphetamine or ecstasy or Molly) is similar to mescaline and also known as one of the 'club drugs' or ‘rave drug’ . It is a stimulant with hallucinogenic properties, supposed to interact with serotoninergic neurons in the CNS (typical effects include increased empathy and sensory perception). It is less likely to cause marked agitation and mydriasis compared to cocaine.
  • • Option A
  • • MDMA
  • • Ecstasy
  • • mescaline
  • • one of the 'club drugs' or ‘rave drug’
  • • Option B . Marijuana : Marijuana use is associated with relaxation , euphoria , and altered perception . It is unlikely to cause the marked agitation and sympathetic overactivity seen in the patient.
  • • Option B
  • • Marijuana
  • • associated with relaxation
  • • euphoria
  • • altered perception
  • • Option D . Heroin : Heroin is an opioid and typically causes sedation , respiratory depression, and constricted pupils (miosis). It does not typically produce the sympathetic overactivity seen with cocaine use.
  • • Option
  • • D
  • • Heroin
  • • opioid and typically causes sedation
  • • respiratory depression, and constricted pupils

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The characteristic clinical features of acute cocaine intoxication , includes agitation, mydriasis, elevated vital signs, and profuse sweating.

Carbon monoxide poisoning is usually suspected when there is exposure to sources like faulty heating systems.
